rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is supported via explicit parent context: parent_statement says "Improve energy and carbon efficiency across ... buildings, households". The policy_statement/verbatim_text is "Introduce smart home technology" under "Household", which plausibly targets household energy efficiency (consistent with canonical mechanism "smart_home_technology_adoption → residential_energy_use_reduction"). Gate failure cites missing outcome token, but the intervention remains resource/energy efficiency aligned in the given package context.
mechanism_description: Introduce smart home technology to help households monitor and optimize energy use, reducing residential energy consumption and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (heating/cooling/appliances/lighting) → improved control/automation + behavior feedback → ↓ energy input → ↓ fuel combustion & electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: Households adopt smart home devices and energy management systems (e.g., smart thermostats, smart plugs, home energy displays, automated controls) that adjust settings, schedule loads, and provide real-time feedback.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced end-use energy demand (less electricity and/or less onsite fuel use) through optimized HVAC operation, reduced standby loads, peak/load shifting, and avoided unnecessary consumption.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational GHG emissions from power generation and/or onsite combustion (and potentially reduced upstream fuel-cycle emissions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering residential energy use for the same household services, thereby reducing combustion- and grid-related GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Smart home tech → reduced residential energy input for the same services → reduced energy supply requirements → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The direct, intended, and typically largest effect is energy efficiency/demand reduction (Resource Efficiency). While emissions reduction is the outcome, the mechanism is fundamentally “same service with less energy,” not primarily fuel switching or sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If smart tech is used mainly for demand response without net energy reduction, the mitigation benefit may come from grid optimization; however, the dominant pathway across this mechanism as stated is reduced consumption. If the technology is installed mainly for resilience (e.g., outage management), that would be Adaptation, but that is not implied here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is smart-home adoption leading to reduced residential energy use fits Resource Efficiency because the core lever is demand reduction via control/optimization.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy is best subtyped as efficiency, demand response, electrification-enabling, or behavior change depends on policy design and evidence at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations emphasize peak shifting or demand response (grid flexibility) more than total kWh reduction; rebound effects (greater comfort/usage) can erode savings; climate benefit depends on marginal grid emissions and whether reduced demand displaces fossil generation.
co_benefits: Improved household comfort; reduced energy bills; peak load reduction/grid reliability; potential air quality improvements from reduced energy generation
instance_edge_case_notes: The text does not specify the policy lever (e.g., rebate program vs. building code requirement vs. information campaign). Classified as an incentive/enabling program by default because “introduce” implies promoting uptake rather than mandating installation.
